diagrams.title: Monolithic OS Structure
diagrams.description: A rectangular container representing the entire kernel space, with internal divisions showing the main components: process management, memory management, file system, device drivers, and I/O management all packed together without clear separation. The user applications sit outside at the top. This diagram illustrates how early operating systems like early UNIX were structured as a single large kernel where all OS services reside in one address space.
elements: Internal labeled sections for: Process Management, Memory Management, File System, Device Drivers, I/O System
User Applications layer above the kernel
Arrow showing system calls from applications to kernel
diagrams.purpose: Explains the monolithic kernel architecture where all OS services run in a single kernel address space, providing context for understanding the simplicity and performance benefits but also the lack of isolation between components.

diagrams.title: Layered OS Structure
diagrams.description: A vertical stack of horizontal layers, each contained in its own box, showing the hierarchical organization from hardware at the bottom to user applications at the top. The layers include: Hardware, CPU Scheduling/Process Management, Memory Management, Device Management, I/O缓冲ing, File System, and User Interface, with each layer only interacting with the layer immediately below it.
elements: Hardware layer (bottom) - CPU, Memory, I/O devices
Vertical arrows showing communication between adjacent layers only
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the layered approach to OS design where the system is divided into hierarchical levels, each building on the services provided by the layer below, promoting modularity, ease of implementation, and maintainability.

diagrams.title: Microkernel OS Structure
diagrams.description: A small central rectangle representing the minimal microkernel at the core, surrounded by multiple separate user-space server processes. The microkernel contains only essential functions: IPC, basic scheduling, and low-level memory management. Separate boxes for file server, process server, device driver server, and network server run in user space. User applications connect to these servers through message passing via the microkernel.
elements: Small central rectangle - Microkernel (IPC, basic scheduling, memory management)
Horizontal arrows showing message passing between servers and microkernel
Dashed lines showing applications communicating with servers
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the microkernel architecture where only essential core functions run in kernel space, while OS services like file systems and device drivers run as separate user-space processes, improving reliability, security, and extensibility through message passing.
